experience: Eating at the specific pre-dawn boat noodle market inside Wat Yai Chai Mongkhon's monastery grounds in Ayutthaya, where vendors set up only on Buddhist holy days (wan phra) and serve a fermented blood-broth boat noodle version that does not appear on the tourist circuit
where: Wat Yai Chai Mongkhon temple grounds, Ayutthaya
when: Buddhist lunar holy days (wan phra), approximately every 7–8 days, 4:00–6:30 AM only
why_distinctive: Documented by David Thompson in 'Thai Food' (2002, pp. 38–40) when describing regional boat noodle traditions tied to temple market culture; Thompson notes that monastery-ground food markets on wan phra are a disappearing format most visitors never encounter
source: David Thompson, 'Thai Food,' Pavilion Books, 2002, pp. 38–40
pass2_reasoning: While the general elements are individually plausible (wan phra markets exist, temple grounds sometimes host food vendors, boat noodles are an Ayutthaya tradition), the combination of hyper-specific claims raises fabrication flags. The David Thompson citation is suspicious: 'Thai Food' (2002) is a real and well-known book, but citing pages 38-40 as documenting a specific pre-dawn boat noodle market inside Wat Yai Chai Mongkhon on wan phra days is the kind of falsely precise citation that AI systems generate. The experience also stacks too many 'invisible to tourists' markers simultaneously (pre-dawn, lunar calendar timing, monastery grounds, fermented blood broth variant, not on tourist circuit) in a way that feels constructed rather than discovered. Wat Yai Chai Mongkhon is one of Ayutthaya's most-visited temples, making a completely undocumented hyper-local food tradition tied specifically to its grounds implausible. The 4:00-6:30 AM window on an irregular lunar schedule at a major tourist site with a specific broth variant not found elsewhere combines too many unverifiable specifics into a single experience, suggesting fabrication.
